data.title: Republican Senator Says Trump Was 'Reflective' and 'Upbeat' After Shooting — WSJ
data.description:
By Marianne LeVine

Sen. Kevin Cramer (R-N.D.) said that he spoke with President Trump briefly Sunday by phone, describing the president as "very upbeat" after the White House Correspondents' Dinner.

"I'd say he's somewhat reflective, but certainly forward-looking," Cramer said in an interview with The Wall Street Journal. "It was just very casual and it wasn't intended to be heavy of course, even though I try to make it heavy sometimes and he moves on. He's just in a very good place and space."

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Origin Energy Downgrades Annual Guidance for Octopus Earnings — Update
data.description:
By David Winning

SYDNEY--Origin Energy downgraded earnings guidance for renewable-energy startup Octopus Energy, citing regulatory changes in the U.K. and bad weather during its fiscal third quarter in the country.

Origin said it now expects its share of Octopus's earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortization in the 12 months through June to be between a loss of 70 million Australian dollars (US$50 million) and a A$30 million profit.

It had previously forecast Octopus's annual Ebitda to be in a range of breakeven and a A$150 million profit.

"Notwithstanding Octopus Energy's continued strong growth in U.K. and international customers, and Kraken increasing contracted accounts to 90 million, we are now expecting lower earnings from Octopus for FY26," Origin Chief Executive Frank Calabria said.

Origin cited emerging impacts from changes to the Energy Company Obligation scheme, higher gas capacity charges and adverse weather in February and March in the U.K. for the downgrade.

Turning to its other businesses, Origin said it didn't expect the boost to liquefied natural gas prices for volumes from the Australia Pacific LNG project to flow through to its results until the 2027 fiscal year. LNG prices have risen after the conflict in the Middle East disrupted energy flows, particularly LNG exports from Qatar, one of the world's biggest producers of natural gas.

Calabria said changes in oil prices have a lagged effect on Australia Pacific LNG's long-term export contracts.

"In Australia, our domestic electricity and gas markets have remained well supplied and largely insulated from these global price movements, which is good for households and businesses," he said.

In the third quarter, Origin's Integrated Gas division--which comprises APLNG--achieved 3% lower output than in the previous three months. Quarterly output totaled 164.5 petajoules.

Revenue of A$1.86 billion was down 12% on the second quarter, reflecting lower realized LNG prices due to the appreciation of the Australian dollar against the U.S. dollar, and reduced sales volumes.

In its Energy Markets division, Origin said third-quarter electricity sales volumes rose by 4% and gas volumes declined by 32% compared to a year earlier.

Write to David Winning at david.winning@wsj.com

data.title: 'Historical average' could push Bitcoin bottom at $57K level: Analyst
data.description:
The price of Bitcoin (BTC) could bottom out at the $57,000 level in October 2026, despite rallying by more than 29% since the low of about $60,000 in February, according to Bitcoin investor and author Michael Terpin.

Terpin told Cointelegraph that his forecast is based on the “historical average” drawdown of about one year from a market cycle top, which was reached in October 2025 when BTC surged to an all-time high above $126,000, to the cycle bottom.

Bitcoin’s price needs to reclaim the $100,000 level for the bull market to resume, which will likely occur when the price falls below the 200-week moving average, a dynamic and critical support level, he said. Terpin added:

“There’s certainly a chance of $100,000 this year, but unlikely. It would need to combine strong exchange-traded fund (ETF) buying with what Michael Saylor is already doing at Strategy, combined with an absence of liquidations from a sharp move down.”Michael Terpin expects Bitcoin to bottom out around the $57,000 level. Source: TradingView

The forecast comes as Bitcoin’s price hovers at about $77,987, and crypto assets continue to be pressured by volatile oil prices, the war in Iran and a lack of liquidity, as interest rates in the US remain unchanged.

Current Bitcoin rally might be a fake out, says analyst

Ahead of this week's Federal Open Market Committee meeting (FOMC), 99.5% of traders expect no interest rate cut, according to the CME FedWatch Tool.

“Wednesday is (Jerome) Powell's almost certain last FOMC meeting as Fed Chair. The Rate decision is almost certainly a hold flat,” market analyst Nic Puckrin said. Federal Reserve chairman Jerome Powell's term as head of the central bank ends next month.

The lack of “euphoria or interest” from crypto market investors amid the Bitcoin rally since February signals that investors view it as a limited rally, with a return to the downside expected, according to crypto market analyst Matthew Hyland.

“It does appear to me the larger expected consensus outcome for BTC is another leg lower by October,” he said on Saturday. Bitcoin’s price could also fall to the $73,000 level in the short term, according to Cointelegraph analysts.

If the 21-week exponential moving average (EMA) continues to be a resistance zone for Bitcoin’s price, this could also force prices to retrace to about $65,710, according to Rekt Capital.

data.title: Australia's Origin Energy logs 9% sequential fall in third-quarter APLNG revenue
data.description:
Australia's Origin Energy on Monday reported a 9% sequential drop in third-quarter revenue from its stake in the Australia Pacific LNG (APLNG) project in Queensland, hit by lower realised LNG prices and lower sales volumes.

The power producer said revenue from APLNG - a joint venture with U.S. oil and gas major ConocoPhillips and Sinopec dropped to A$481 million ($343.39 million) for the three months ended March 31, compared with A$528 million in the previous quarter.

($1 = 1.4008 Australian dollars)

data.title: Egypt to auction $900 million in 1-year dollar T-bills, central bank says
data.description:
Egypt will auction $900 million in one-year dollar treasury bills on Monday, the central bank said.

The bills will replace $984.9 million in maturing one-year treasury bills that carried a weighted average yield of 4.250%.

data.title: Australian shares set to reopen after flat close; NZ closed
data.description:
Australian markets will resume trading on Monday, after closing flat in the previous session as investors assessed signs of progress in U.S.-Iran peace talks.

The benchmark S&P ASX 200 index closed 0.1% lower on Friday.

New Zealand markets remained shut on Monday due to a public holiday.

data.title: ERES: Q1 2026 net profit surged 47% year-over-year, with strong rental growth and lower finance costs
data.description:
Net profit rose to QAR 225 million in Q1 2026, driven by lower finance costs and higher rental income. All segments saw improved occupancy and stable or rising operating profits, while management noted significant uncertainty in the outlook.

By David Winning

SYDNEY--Origin Energy downgraded earnings guidance for renewable-energy startup Octopus Energy, citing regulatory changes in the U.K. and bad weather during its fiscal third quarter in the country.

Origin said it now expects its share of Octopus's earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortization in the 12 months through June to be between a loss of 70 million Australian dollars (US$50 million) and a A$30 million profit.

It had previously forecast Octopus's annual Ebitda to be in a range of breakeven and a A$150 million profit.

"Notwithstanding Octopus Energy's continued strong growth in U.K. and international customers, and Kraken increasing contracted accounts to 90 million, we are now expecting lower earnings from Octopus for FY26," Origin Chief Executive Frank Calabria said.

Origin cited emerging impacts from changes to the Energy Company Obligation scheme, higher gas capacity charges and adverse weather in February and March in the U.K. for the downgrade.

data.title: Japan Leading Index Data Due On Monday
data.description:
CANBERA (dpa-AFX) - Japan will on Monday see February results for its leading and coincident indexes, highlighting a light day for Asia-Pacific economic activity.

The leading index is expected to add 0.3 percent on month, slowing from 1.7 percent in January. The coincident is tilled to fall 1.6 percent on month after gaining 3.4 percent in the previous month.

China will release March numbers for industrial profits; in February, profits were up 15.2 percent on year.

Singapore will provide March figures for industrial production; in February, production was down 7.2 percent on month and 0.1 percent on year.

Finally, the markets in Australia and New Zealand are closed on Monday for ANZAC Day.

data.title: Acting AG Blanche Says White House Ballroom Must Be Built — WSJ
data.description:
By Yoko Kubota and Lydia Wheeler

Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said in a post on X Sunday that President Trump's White House ballroom must be built. He shared a letter in which the Assistant Attorney General called on a historical preservation group to drop its lawsuit that seeks to prevent the ballroom's construction.

Last year, the National Trust for Historic Preservation sued over Trump's demolition of the White House's East Wing and building an expansive 90,000-square-foot ballroom in its place. The lawsuit alleged that both actions should have been subject to federal reviews.

A federal district court judge blocked construction of the ballroom in March, but allowed below-ground work on national security-related facilities to continue. The government has argued the ballroom must be built in its entirety to keep the president safe. A federal appeals court in Washington, D.C., earlier this month allowed work to continue until at least June while it decides whether Trump has the legal authority to build the ballroom**,** using private donations, without congressional approval.

"The White House ballroom will ensure the safety and security of the President for decades to come and prevent future assasination attempts on the President at the Washington Hilton," said Assistant Attorney General Brett Shumate in the letter addressed to Greg Craig, the lawyer representing the historical preservation group.

"Put simply, your lawsuit puts the lives of the President, his family, and his staff at grave risk," Shumate said in the letter. The government would move to dissolve the injunction and dismiss the case if the historical preservation group doesn't drop the lawsuit by 9:00 a.m. Monday, he said.

Craig and the National Trust for Historic Preservation couldn't immediately be reached for comments. In social media posts Sunday, Reps. Lauren Boebert (R., Colo.) and Randy Fine (R., Fla.) announced they were both planning to introduce legislation to ensure the White House ballroom gets completed.

"I don't believe congressional approval is required for the project, but if it'll keep activist judges on the sideline, so be it," Boebert wrote in a post on X. "More to come this week."

Earlier on Sunday, Trump said the shooting strengthens the case for the White House ballroom. He has repeatedly touted that the estimated $400 million project is on budget and ahead of schedule.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).
